description: The party stew. Beef, tripe, ponmo, kidney, and chicken all in one deeply fried tomato base. Every cut brings a different texture and flavour — the complexity is the point. This is the stew people ask for the recipe.

ingredients.name: beef chunks (chuck or brisket)
ingredients.note: The base meat. Chuck or brisket — tough cuts that become tender and rich after long cooking.

ingredients.name: beef tripe (shaki)
ingredients.note: Takes the longest to cook. Must be parboiled separately until fully tender before joining the stew. Has a distinctive chewy texture that many love.

ingredients.name: ponmo (cow skin)
ingredients.note: Must be pre-cooked until soft. Absorbs stew flavour beautifully and adds a gelatinous richness to the sauce.

ingredients.name: beef kidney
ingredients.note: Cook separately — brief parboil, then fry. Strong flavour that enriches the base. Remove the white membrane before cooking.

ingredients.name: chicken pieces (thighs/drumsticks)
ingredients.note: Adds a lighter contrast to the heavy beef cuts. Parboil separately as it cooks faster.

ingredients.name: plum tomatoes
ingredients.note: More tomatoes for a larger, more complex stew. Fresh is best.

ingredients.name: red bell pepper (tatashe)
ingredients.note: More tatashe for deeper colour and sweetness to balance the rich meats.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Assorted stew can handle more heat — the fat from multiple meats absorbs it.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 each for parboiling beef, tripe, and chicken separately, 1 sliced for the stew base.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: Larger quantity for a bigger stew. The assorted meats will also render fat into the pot.

ingredients.name: tomato paste
ingredients.note: More paste for a richer, more concentrated base to carry all the different meat flavours.

ingredients.name: curry powder
ingredients.note: Divided — some for marinating the meats, some for the stew base.

ingredients.name: thyme
ingredients.note: Divided between meat marinades and stew base.

ingredients.name: bay leaves
ingredients.note: Particularly important in assorted stew — adds herbal depth that cuts through the richness of multiple meats.

ingredients.name: seasoning cubes
ingredients.note: Distributed across all the parboiling stages and the stew base.

ingredients.name: garlic
ingredients.note: For marinating the beef and tripe. Highly recommended — the assorted meats can carry the strong garlic flavour.

ingredients.name: ginger
ingredients.note: Particularly helps with the tripe and kidney smell. Use generously in those marinades.

ingredients.name: white pepper
ingredients.note: For the meat marinades.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: Essential for depth in assorted stew — it bridges all the different meat flavours.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season each meat separately during parboiling. Season the stew base carefully at the end — the collective stock is very salty.

ingredients.name: combined meat stock
ingredients.note: Combine the stocks from all the different meats. This blended stock is the most flavourful liquid you can put in a stew.

steps.title: Prepare and Marinate All Meats
steps.instruction: Clean all meats separately. For tripe: scrub thoroughly with salt and lime juice, rinse well. For kidney: remove the white membrane and any tubes, soak in salted water for 10 minutes. Season each meat type separately: beef and tripe with curry, thyme, garlic, ginger, white pepper, seasoning cube, and salt. Chicken with curry, thyme, and seasoning. Kidney lightly with just salt and pepper. Rest all meats for at least 30 minutes.
steps.sensory_cue: All meats should smell clean and well-seasoned. Tripe especially should smell neutral after proper cleaning — any strong ammonia smell means it needs more washing.
steps.failure_risk: Improperly cleaned tripe or kidney will give the entire stew an off-putting smell. Take the cleaning seriously. Season each meat type separately because they have different cooking times and flavour intensities.

steps.title: Parboil Tripe Separately (Longest Cook)
steps.instruction: Place seasoned tripe in a pot with 1 chopped onion, bay leaf, and water to cover. Cook on high heat until fully tender — tripe should offer no resistance when pressed and cut easily with a spoon. This can take 60–90 minutes. Drain and cut into bite-sized pieces. Reserve the stock — it is intensely flavoured.
steps.sensory_cue: Properly cooked tripe is soft, slightly translucent, and cuts easily. The stock will be very dark and strongly flavoured. The smell will be earthy and rich.
steps.failure_risk: Undercooked tripe is unpleasantly rubbery. It cannot be fixed in the stew later — there is not enough time or liquid. Get it fully tender here.

steps.title: Parboil Beef and Ponmo Together
steps.instruction: In a second pot, cook beef chunks and ponmo with 1 chopped onion, bay leaf, and water to barely cover. Cook on medium-high until beef is 75% tender and ponmo is completely soft. The ponmo will be done first — remove it and let the beef continue. Reserve the stock.
steps.sensory_cue: Beef at 75%: yields to fork but has slight resistance. Ponmo: completely soft, slightly gelatinous, translucent. The combined stock is rich and dark.
steps.failure_risk: Overcooked ponmo disintegrates in the stew. Watch it — it softens faster than the beef. Remove it as soon as it yields.

steps.title: Parboil Chicken and Fry Kidney
steps.instruction: Parboil chicken in a small amount of water with seasoning until 80% cooked. Reserve the stock. Separately, heat a little oil in a small pan over high heat. Fry kidney pieces for 2 minutes each side until browned. Kidney is strong — frying it briefly reduces its intensity and creates a better texture. Set all aside.
steps.sensory_cue: Chicken: mostly cooked through. Kidney: browned exterior, still slightly pink inside — it will finish in the stew. It should smell savoury, not pungent.
steps.failure_risk: Overcooked chicken at this stage will fall off the bone and disintegrate in the stew. Stop at 80%. Kidney overcooks to rubbery quickly — 2 minutes per side maximum in the frying step.

steps.title: Fry All Meats
steps.instruction: Heat half the oil in a large heavy pot over high heat. Fry all meat types in batches — beef first (takes longest to colour), then chicken, then tripe, then ponmo. Fry each batch until golden-brown. Do not rush or crowd. Remove each batch as it is done. The pot bottom will collect incredible caramelised meat fond — do not clean it.
steps.sensory_cue: Each meat type should have a golden-brown sear. The pot bottom should look dark and caramelised — this is concentrated flavour that will dissolve into the stew base. The kitchen smell at this point is extraordinary.
steps.failure_risk: Skipping the frying step for assorted stew is not an option. The sear on each cut gives the stew its signature depth. Without it you have a boiled meat stew, not an assorted stew.

steps.title: Build and Fry the Stew Base
steps.instruction: In the same pot with all the meat drippings, add remaining oil if needed. Fry sliced onions until deep golden. Add tomato paste and fry 4 minutes — it should darken significantly. Pour in blended tomatoes, tatashe, and scotch bonnet. Fry over high heat, stirring every 90 seconds, for 25–30 minutes until very thick, deeply coloured, and the oil has risen clearly to the surface. This base has to be bold enough to carry all the meats.
steps.sensory_cue: The base should look almost like a thick paste — very dark brick-red. Oil floating on top. All the caramelised meat fond from the bottom of the pot should have dissolved into the base, giving it a deeper, more complex colour and smell than a simple tomato base.
steps.failure_risk: This is the largest batch of base in any of these stew packs. It needs the full time. Rushing it leaves a watery, sour base that cannot hold up against this volume of meat.

steps.title: Season and Add Combined Stock
steps.instruction: Reduce to medium heat. Add curry powder, thyme, ground crayfish, bay leaves, and crushed seasoning cubes. Season with salt — carefully, the combined stock is already very salty. Stir well. Combine all reserved stocks (tripe, beef, chicken) and pour in 2 cups of this combined stock. Bring to a rolling simmer. Taste — it should be deeply complex, almost overwhelming in richness.
steps.sensory_cue: This seasoned base with combined stock is the most flavourful liquid in any of these stew recipes. The layers — tomato, crayfish, multiple meat stocks, spices — should be clearly distinct on your palate.
steps.failure_risk: Taste before adding salt. The combined multi-meat stock is concentrated. Adding full seasoning without tasting first is how you end up with an oversalted stew you cannot fix.

steps.title: Add All Meats and Finish
steps.instruction: Add all the fried meats back into the stew base — beef first, then tripe, ponmo, kidney, and chicken last. Stir gently to coat everything. Cover and simmer on low heat for 25–30 minutes, stirring gently every 8 minutes. The stew should thicken around all the meats, each piece coated in the rich base. Remove bay leaves. Final taste and salt adjustment. Let rest 10 minutes before serving.
steps.sensory_cue: Every piece of meat should look deeply coated and glossy. The stew should be thick, rich, and complex — not watery, not oily. Each meat type should be identifiable by texture. The smell should be layered, bold, and unmistakably Nigerian party food.
steps.failure_risk: Stirring too aggressively at this stage breaks the more delicate cuts. Gentle folding only. If the stew reduces too much and becomes too thick, add a splash of the remaining stock. This stew improves significantly after sitting for an hour.
